questions.stem: In an LLM interview, what is a token and why does tokenization matter in practice?
options: Tokens are subword/char pieces models consume; they affect cost, length, and splitting
Tokens are entire PDF files ingested as a single embedding vector always
Tokenization only matters for images and is unused in text Transformers
A token is always exactly one English word with spaces preserved
questions.explanation: Models operate on token IDs; vocabulary choices change length and meaning boundaries.

questions.stem: Context window: what constraint should you explain clearly to interviewers about LLMs?
options: The number of GPUs required to host the model weights in memory
Max tokens of prompt+generation the model can attend to in one forward pass
How many users can call the API concurrently before rate limits
The count of fine-tuning epochs allowed before catastrophic forgetting
questions.explanation: Context length caps how much text the model can condition on at once.

questions.stem: Pretraining versus fine-tuning: how do you distinguish the two stages cleanly?
options: Fine-tuning always uses more data than pretraining by definition
Pretraining requires labeled instructions only; fine-tuning is unsupervised only
Pretrain on broad data for general skills; fine-tune adapts to a narrower task/domain
They are identical stages with different cloud product marketing names
questions.explanation: Pretraining builds foundations; fine-tuning specializes behavior or knowledge.

questions.stem: RLHF at a high level: what problem is it trying to solve for assistant models?
options: Replace all human feedback with purely unsupervised clustering of prompts
Guarantee factual correctness on every knowledge question without retrieval
Compress model weights to INT4 without changing any behavior
Align model outputs with human preference judgments beyond next-token likelihood
questions.explanation: Preference optimization steers models toward helpful/harmless preferred answers.

questions.stem: LoRA fine-tuning: what efficiency idea should you state in an interview answer?
options: Train low-rank adapter matrices while keeping most base weights frozen
Retrain every parameter from scratch on a tiny dataset only
Delete attention layers and fine-tune only the tokenizer vocabulary
LoRA quantizes gradients to 1-bit and freezes the optimizer state
questions.explanation: LoRA adds small trainable ranks, cutting fine-tune cost and storage.

questions.stem: Temperature in decoding: what happens as you increase it, conceptually speaking?
options: It always selects the single argmax token regardless of probabilities
Distribution flattens so sampling becomes more random/diverse (and riskier)
Temperature only changes GPU clock speed during inference serving
Higher temperature guarantees higher factual accuracy on closed quizzes
questions.explanation: Temperature scales logits before softmax, controlling randomness.

questions.stem: Top-p (nucleus) sampling: what does the method keep when sampling next tokens?
options: Exactly the top p tokens by count, ignoring their probability masses
Only tokens that appear in the prompt verbatim at least p times
The smallest set of top tokens whose cumulative probability mass exceeds p
All tokens equally after truncating the vocabulary to size p
questions.explanation: Nucleus sampling truncates the tail while adapting to distribution shape.

questions.stem: Hallucination in LLMs: what definition and mitigation angle should you give?
options: Any output that uses a rare word not seen in the pretraining corpus
Only syntax errors produced when temperature is set to zero
Hallucinations are impossible once RLHF is applied to any model
Fluent but unfounded content; mitigate via grounding, refusal, and evals
questions.explanation: Models can invent facts; retrieval and verification reduce risk.

questions.stem: Prompt injection: what attack pattern should you describe in security interviews?
options: Malicious instructions in content try to override system/developer policies
Injecting SQL into a database through the tokenizer vocabulary file
Increasing temperature until the model refuses all user requests
Fine-tuning on public Common Crawl to improve factuality only
questions.explanation: Untrusted text can hijack instruction-following if not isolated/filtered.

questions.stem: Quantization for LLMs: what tradeoff are interviewers usually probing for here?
options: Quantization increases parameter count while improving perplexity always
Lower bit-width weights cut memory/latency with some quality risk
It only changes tokenizer BPE merges and never touches weight tensors
INT8 models cannot run on GPUs and require CPUs exclusively
questions.explanation: Compressing numeric precision enables cheaper serving with quality checks.

questions.stem: System prompt versus user prompt: why does the distinction matter in products?
options: User prompts always outrank system prompts by Transformer architecture law
System prompts are only used for image models, not text LLMs
System/developer messages set policy; user content should not freely override it
There is no operational difference; both strings are concatenated identically
questions.explanation: Privilege separation in prompting supports safety and product behavior.

questions.stem: Perplexity as an LM metric: what does lower perplexity generally indicate about fit?
options: The model hallucinates less on every open-domain fact question
Latency is lower because fewer tokens are generated per second
The context window has grown without changing model weights
The model assigns higher probability to the evaluation text (better fit)
questions.explanation: Perplexity summarizes how surprised the model is by held-out text.

questions.stem: Instruction tuning: what data and objective shift does it typically involve?
options: Supervised examples of instructions→helpful responses to improve following
Only continuing unsupervised next-token training on raw web text
Replacing the tokenizer with character-level encoding exclusively
Maximizing temperature during training to increase entropy forever
questions.explanation: Instruction data teaches models to respond to directives more reliably.

questions.stem: Few-shot prompting: what mechanism are you relying on without weight updates?
options: Automatically downloading and fine-tuning a LoRA from the examples
In-context examples steer the model’s next-token behavior for the task
Updating embeddings of every vocabulary item using the shots
Few-shot requires RLHF reward models to be retrained online
questions.explanation: Demonstrations in the prompt exploit in-context learning abilities.

questions.stem: KV cache during autoregressive decoding: why do serving stacks keep it around?
options: Store the entire training dataset in GPU RAM for nearest-neighbor lookup
Cache gradient checkpoints so training can resume mid-epoch only
Reuse key/value attention states for past tokens to avoid recomputing them
Memorize user passwords for personalized decoding without prompts
questions.explanation: KV caching makes incremental token generation computationally feasible.

questions.stem: Beam search versus sampling: when might you choose each at decode time?
options: Beam search always maximizes creativity better than any sampler
Sampling cannot be used for chat; beam is required for all LLMs
They produce identical outputs given the same random seed always
Beam for more deterministic high-likelihood text; sampling for diversity
questions.explanation: Search vs stochastic decoding trades mode-seeking against variety.

questions.stem: Tokenizer fertility / multilingual issues: what practical failure can occur?
options: Some languages/scripts consume many more tokens for the same text length
Tokenizers always allocate exactly one token per Unicode code point
Multilingual text cannot be tokenized by any BPE vocabulary
Fertility only affects image patch embeddings, not text models
questions.explanation: Uneven tokenization changes cost and effective context per language.

questions.stem: Catastrophic forgetting in fine-tuning: what risk should you call out clearly?
options: Forgetting only happens when learning rates are exactly zero
Narrow fine-tuning can degrade broad capabilities the base model had
LoRA mathematically prevents any change to model behavior forever
Pretrained models cannot forget because attention is immutable
questions.explanation: Task adaptation may overwrite useful prior skills without care.

questions.stem: Base model versus chat model: what difference do product interviews expect?
options: Base models refuse all prompts; chat models only complete code
They share identical post-training and differ only in API URL paths
Chat/instruct models are aligned to dialogue; base models continue raw text
Chat models cannot use tools; base models must use tools always
questions.explanation: Post-training changes interaction style and safety behavior substantially.

questions.stem: Why does greedy decoding (temp≈0 / argmax) still sometimes look wrong in answers?
options: Greedy decoding ignores the prompt and samples uniformly from vocab
Argmax is undefined for softmax outputs in modern frameworks
Temperature zero always triggers prompt injection by mathematical law
Highest next-token probability ≠ globally best sequence or factual truth
questions.explanation: Locally optimal tokens can yield globally suboptimal or false answers.

questions.stem: SFT then preference optimization: why is this pipeline common for assistants?
options: SFT teaches format/skills; preference methods refine choices among responses
Preference optimization must precede any supervised instruction tuning always
SFT deletes the need for evaluation because loss equals user satisfaction
The pipeline only applies to encoder-only BERT-style classifiers
questions.explanation: Stage training first imitates demos, then ranks better answers via feedback.

questions.stem: DPO versus classic RLHF with a separate reward model — key conceptual difference?
options: DPO requires a live human rater on every token during decoding
DPO can optimize preferences directly from pairs without an explicit reward model loop
Classic RLHF forbids any preference data and uses only next-token loss
They are bitwise identical algorithms with different paper titles
questions.explanation: DPO simplifies preference learning relative to full RL with RM+PPO-style loops.

questions.stem: QLoRA: what combination makes fine-tuning large models more accessible in practice?
options: Full FP32 fine-tuning of all layers on a single laptop CPU overnight
Deleting half the layers randomly before each training step
Quantized frozen base weights plus low-rank adapters trained in higher precision
Training only the tokenizer while freezing embeddings and attention
questions.explanation: 4-bit bases + LoRA cut memory so larger models fit consumer GPUs.

questions.stem: Speculative decoding: what latency idea should you explain at a high level?
options: Speculate user intent without reading the prompt to skip attention
Generate the entire answer offline before the user sends a message
Replace sampling with hashing into a fixed FAQ database
A cheap draft model proposes tokens; the large model verifies them in bulk
questions.explanation: Draft-then-verify can reduce wall-clock time for autoregressive decoding.

questions.stem: Rope / relative position schemes: why do long-context models care about them?
options: Position methods affect how well attention extrapolates beyond train lengths
They replace the need for any attention mechanism in long documents
Relative positions only matter for vision CNNs, not language models
They guarantee perfect retrieval of facts from pretraining memorization
questions.explanation: Positional inductive biases influence long-context generalization.

questions.stem: Constitutional AI / principle-based critique: what alignment idea does it capture?
options: Hard-code a legal constitution into GPU microcode for every inference
Use written principles and model self-critique to improve harmlessness/helpfulness
Ban all system prompts so only user messages can set policy
Replace tokenization with rule-based keyword filters exclusively
questions.explanation: Principle-guided feedback reduces reliance on exhaustive human labeling.

questions.stem: Eval harness pitfalls: why can leaderboard gains fail to transfer to your app?
options: All public evals are adversarially encrypted against commercial models
Leaderboards measure only FLOPs and never measure accuracy
Benchmark overfitting, contamination, and mismatched task distributions
App metrics are illegal to define differently from MMLU by policy
questions.explanation: Good public scores may not match your domain, prompts, or risk profile.

questions.stem: Tool-calling / function calling: what does the model output to invoke tools?
options: Raw GPU assembly that directly mutates production databases
Only natural language with no machine-parseable structure allowed
A full fine-tune checkpoint uploaded mid-conversation automatically
Structured call intents (name+args) that a runtime executes and returns
questions.explanation: The model proposes calls; orchestration runs tools and feeds results back.

questions.stem: Prompt caching: what is being reused to save cost/latency on long prefixes?
options: Precomputed KV (or equivalent) state for a repeated system/prompt prefix
Cached gradient descent steps from a previous fine-tune job
Browser cookies storing the model’s private training data shards
A permanent freeze of temperature and top-p across all users
questions.explanation: Reusable prompt prefixes avoid recomputing attention over static context.

questions.stem: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs: what scaling idea should you mention in interviews?
options: Every token activates every parameter on every layer without routing
Route tokens to sparse expert FFNs so total params grow with less compute/token
MoE replaces attention with a single linear layer shared globally
Experts are human specialists who edit outputs before returning
questions.explanation: Conditional computation increases capacity without full dense cost.

questions.stem: Safety refusal vs over-refusal: what product tension should you articulate clearly?
options: Refuse all user questions to guarantee zero safety incidents forever
Never refuse because helpfulness always outranks safety policies
Block harmful requests without needlessly refusing benign edge queries
Over-refusal only occurs when quantization bits drop below eight
questions.explanation: Calibration of refusal boundaries is a core alignment/product tradeoff.

questions.stem: Embedding models vs generative LLMs: how do their jobs differ in ML stacks?
options: Embedders always decode full paragraphs; generators only output vectors
They are the same checkpoint used with different API path names
Generators cannot be instruction-tuned; embedders must use RLHF
Embedders map text to vectors for retrieval/similarity; generators produce text
questions.explanation: Different objectives: similarity spaces versus next-token generation.

questions.stem: Context stuffing limits: besides token caps, what quality issue appears in long prompts?
options: Lost-in-the-middle and distraction; more tokens ≠ better use of evidence
Longer prompts always monotonically improve factual accuracy
Models ignore the beginning of prompts and only read the final token
Context stuffing removes the need for any retrieval system forever
questions.explanation: Attention allocation can fail to utilize long noisy contexts well.

questions.stem: Continuous pretraining / domain adaptive pretraining: when is it justified?
options: When you have only ten labeled chat turns and no unlabeled text
Large domain corpora where specialized distribution shift hurts a base model
Only to increase temperature defaults for creative writing modes
Whenever you want to avoid evaluation on domain-specific test sets
questions.explanation: More in-domain tokens can shift the base distribution before SFT.

questions.stem: Jailbreaks: how do they relate to prompt injection and safety training goals?
options: Physical break-ins to GPU datacenters to steal model weights only
Automatic fixes that permanently patch all vulnerabilities after one try
Adversarial prompts that try to bypass refusals and safety policies
A decoding mode that sets top-p to zero and temperature to one
questions.explanation: Jailbreaks probe alignment robustness against policy circumvention.

questions.stem: Logprobs / constrained decoding: when are they useful in production LLM APIs?
options: Logprobs replace the need for any tokenizer in modern serving stacks
Constrained decoding forbids JSON and only allows free-form poetry
They only apply during pretraining and cannot be used at inference
Enforce schemas, grammar, or candidate sets; score alternatives with logprobs
questions.explanation: Structure constraints and token scores improve reliability of outputs.

questions.stem: Multimodal LLMs: what extra challenge appears beyond text-only Transformers?
options: Aligning vision/audio encoders with text spaces and cross-modal grounding
Multimodal models cannot use attention and must rely on RNNs only
Images tokenize to fewer tokens than text for the same information
Safety issues disappear because pixels cannot encode instructions
questions.explanation: Cross-modal fusion and grounding add alignment and eval complexity.

questions.stem: Synthetic data for post-training: what risk and benefit should you balance?
options: Synthetic data is always superior to human data with zero downsides
Scale diverse supervision cheaply but watch for artifacts and bias amplification
Models cannot learn from synthetic data by information-theoretic law
Synthetic data only works for embeddings and never for chat models
questions.explanation: Generated data helps coverage but needs filtering and quality controls.

questions.stem: PII leakage from models: what mitigation themes belong in an interview answer?
options: PII cannot leak because Transformers encrypt all memorized strings
Only increase temperature to make memorized PII unreadable
Training data governance, output filters, minimization, and red-team evals
Deleting the system prompt removes all memorization risks forever
questions.explanation: Privacy requires data controls plus runtime and evaluation defenses.

questions.stem: Why might RAG beat fine-tuning for frequently changing enterprise knowledge bases?
options: Fine-tuning always reflects new docs instantly without any retrain cost
RAG cannot cite sources while fine-tuning always provides URLs
Enterprise knowledge is illegal to place in vector indexes by definition
Update documents in the index without retraining weights for each change
questions.explanation: Retrieval keeps knowledge fresh and auditable outside frozen weights.

questions.stem: Senior LLM: how do you structure an evaluation plan beyond a single accuracy number?
options: Task suites, safety, latency/cost, contamination checks, and human spot reviews
Only report training loss because it equals production user satisfaction
One MMLU run replaces all domain and safety evaluations forever
Evaluation is unnecessary after RLHF because preferences guarantee quality
questions.explanation: Multi-axis evals catch capability, risk, and operational regressions.

questions.stem: When would you choose full fine-tuning over LoRA despite higher cost and complexity?
options: Always — LoRA is obsolete and never matches full fine-tune quality
Large domain shift needing broad capacity changes LoRA ranks cannot express
Only when the dataset has fewer than fifty examples total
When you must keep the base weights bit-identical after training
questions.explanation: Full updates help when adapters underfit severe distribution shifts.

questions.stem: Inference-time compute (search/debate/self-consistency): what is the core bet?
options: Delete the model and replace it with a rules engine for hard queries
Reduce context to one token so attention becomes unnecessary
Spend more tokens/compute at decode to raise reliability on hard queries
Inference compute cannot affect quality once weights are frozen
questions.explanation: Test-time scaling trades latency/cost for better reasoned answers.

questions.stem: How should you talk about memorization versus generalization in modern LLMs?
options: LLMs never memorize; every string is recomputed from first principles
Generalization means the model only outputs training set excerpts
Memorization is solved completely by setting temperature above one
Models can regurgitate training strings and also compose novel useful text
questions.explanation: Both verbatim recall and compositional generalization can coexist.

questions.stem: Agentic tool loops vs single-shot completion: when is the multi-step loop justified?
options: When tasks need multi-step gather/act/verify beyond one forward generation
Always, because single-shot completions cannot answer any factual query
Never, because tool use always increases hallucination rates only
Only when the context window is smaller than eight tokens
questions.explanation: Iterative tool use helps tasks that require external state and checks.

questions.stem: Quantization-aware pitfalls: what failure modes should you watch in LLM serving?
options: Quantized models cannot hallucinate by numeric precision limits
Quality cliffs on hard tasks, outlier features, and unstable long generations
INT4 always matches FP16 quality if the tokenizer stays unchanged
Quantization only affects training and never changes inference outputs
questions.explanation: Validate quantized models on realistic prompts, not only perplexity.

questions.stem: Cross-lingual transfer in multilingual LLMs: what caveat belongs in strong answers?
options: All languages always get identical accuracy by architectural symmetry
Multilingual models forbid English prompts by training objective
Performance varies by language resource level and tokenization fertility
Transfer only works for stopwords and not for semantic tasks
questions.explanation: Capability is uneven; evaluate per language rather than assuming parity.

questions.stem: How do you reduce prompt injection risk when tools can take irreversible actions?
options: Put all tool credentials inside the system prompt for convenience
Trust any model-proposed SQL because RLHF prevents harmful queries
Disable logging so injections cannot be detected by attackers
Hard allowlists, confirmations, sandboxing, and separating untrusted content
questions.explanation: Least privilege and human approval gates limit blast radius.

questions.stem: Knowledge cutoff: what product implication must you communicate to stakeholders?
options: Parametric knowledge may be stale; retrieve or browse for fresh facts
Cutoff dates reset automatically every night without engineering work
Fine-tuning once removes cutoff limits for all future world events
Chat models cannot have cutoffs because dialogue is real-time by nature
questions.explanation: Users need grounding strategies when world state changes after training.

questions.stem: Choosing open weights vs hosted API: what decision factors should you list clearly?
options: Open weights always win on safety without any operational tradeoffs
Control, privacy, cost at scale, latency, ops burden, and capability needs
Hosted APIs forbid RAG and tool use by contractual limitation always
The choice depends only on which logo looks better in a pitch deck
questions.explanation: Deployment mode is a systems/product tradeoff, not a single metric.
